name: Aiken
tagline: The most popular smart contract language for Cardano
llmDescription: Aiken is a standalone programming language purpose-built for writing on-chain validator scripts (smart contracts) on Cardano. It compiles to Untyped Plutus Core (UPLC), which is the low-level language executed by Cardano's virtual machine. Aiken does NOT handle off-chain concerns: it does not build transactions, connect to wallets, or query the blockchain. For those tasks you need a separate off-chain SDK (Mesh SDK, Lucid Evolution, Blaze, PyCardano, etc.) that loads the compiled Aiken output (a CIP-57 'blueprint' file called plutus.json). Aiken's syntax is Rust-influenced, statically typed, with pattern matching and algebraic data types. It includes a built-in test runner with property-based testing, a package manager for on-chain library dependencies, LSP for editor support (autocomplete, type checking), and generates CIP-57 blueprint JSON automatically on build. It is the most widely adopted smart contract language in the Cardano ecosystem as of 2025, used by the majority of new DeFi protocols. Aiken focuses exclusively on on-chain validators — it is not a general-purpose language. Developers typically pair Aiken (on-chain) with a TypeScript SDK like Mesh or Blaze (off-chain) as the standard modern Cardano dApp stack.
whenToUse: Use Aiken when you need to write custom on-chain logic (validators/smart contracts) for your Cardano dApp. If your app only sends ADA or mints tokens using native scripts, you do NOT need Aiken — an off-chain SDK is enough. You need Aiken (or another smart contract language) when your app requires conditional spending rules, escrow, DEX logic, lending protocols, or any custom validation beyond simple transfers.
skipUntil: You can skip Aiken entirely if your project only involves sending ADA, minting tokens with native scripts (time-lock, multi-sig), or reading chain data. Many Cardano projects don't need smart contracts at all.
recommendedFor: Any developer who needs smart contracts, regardless of their off-chain language. Especially good for TypeScript developers pairing with Mesh or Blaze.
notRecommendedFor: Python developers who strongly prefer staying in Python (use OpShin instead). Haskell teams who want deep Plutus integration (use Plinth or Plutarch).

name: Plinth (Plutus Tx)
tagline: IOG's official Haskell-based smart contract compiler for Cardano
llmDescription: Plinth (formerly called 'Plutus Tx') is IOG's official Haskell-based smart contract compiler for Cardano. It is a GHC compiler plugin that takes a subset of Haskell code and compiles it to UPLC. Because it uses real Haskell, developers get the full power of Haskell's type system, but it also requires a Haskell development environment (GHC, Cabal/Stack). Plinth generates CIP-57 blueprints and supports Plutus V3 and Conway-era features. IOG is actively developing formal verification tooling specifically for Plinth, using SMT solving and the Lean theorem prover. Plinth does NOT handle off-chain logic — pair it with Atlas (Haskell), CTL (PureScript), or any SDK that can load UPLC/blueprint artifacts. The rename from 'Plutus Tx' to 'Plinth' happened in early 2025 to reduce confusion with the term 'Plutus', which should refer only to the low-level Untyped Plutus Core execution language.
whenToUse: Use when your team is already proficient in Haskell and wants the deepest integration with Cardano's core codebase, or when you need the upcoming formal verification tooling from IOG.
skipUntil: Skip if you're not already a Haskell developer. The learning curve is steep compared to Aiken or OpShin. Setting up a Haskell build environment for Plutus can take significant effort.
recommendedFor: Haskell developers, IOG contributors, teams requiring formal verification of on-chain code.
notRecommendedFor: Developers without Haskell experience. The setup overhead and learning curve is much higher than Aiken.

name: OpShin
tagline: Write Cardano smart contracts in 100% valid Python
llmDescription: OpShin is a smart contract language for Cardano whose syntax is 100% valid Python. You write contracts in normal .py files, use standard Python IDEs, linters, and debuggers, and OpShin compiles them to UPLC. The type system is stricter than Python's (for optimization and security), but any OpShin program can be run and unit-tested with standard pytest. OpShin integrates tightly with PyCardano for off-chain code — the OpShin starter kit uses Ogmios/Kupo or Blockfrost via PyCardano's chain context backends. OpShin compiles to Plutus V3 by default. It can be installed via pip ('pip install opshin') and contracts are compiled with 'opshin build contract.py'. The generated artifacts include a .plutus script file that loads directly as a PyCardano PlutusV3Script. OpShin has a bug bounty funded by Project Catalyst.
whenToUse: Use when you're a Python developer and want to write both on-chain and off-chain code in Python. Also good for data scientists or ML engineers entering the Cardano ecosystem.
skipUntil: Skip if you're not working in Python. Aiken has better tooling (LSP, package manager, property-based testing) and broader ecosystem support.
recommendedFor: Python developers who want a familiar language for smart contracts. The lowest barrier to entry for Pythonistas.
notRecommendedFor: Non-Python developers. Also, OpShin's generated UPLC can be larger than Aiken's — for gas-sensitive production contracts, benchmark both.

name: Plutarch
tagline: Typed Haskell eDSL for writing efficient Plutus Core validators
llmDescription: Plutarch is a typed embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) within Haskell for writing Plutus Core validators. Unlike Plinth, which compiles Haskell to UPLC via a GHC plugin, Plutarch lets you construct UPLC terms directly using Haskell as a meta-language, giving fine-grained control over the generated code. This typically produces smaller, more efficient UPLC than Plinth — critical for DeFi protocols where script execution costs matter. Plutarch requires strong Haskell skills and a deep understanding of how Plutus Core works. It does not generate CIP-57 blueprints natively (you'd generate them separately or use a helper). It is used in production by Liqwid Labs (lending protocol), and the Liqwid-libs monorepo provides helper libraries built on Plutarch.
whenToUse: Use when you need maximum efficiency in your on-chain code (smallest possible script size, lowest execution units) and your team is experienced in Haskell.
skipUntil: Skip unless you're an advanced Haskell developer optimizing production DeFi contracts. Start with Aiken or Plinth first.
recommendedFor: Advanced Haskell developers building gas-optimized DeFi contracts.
notRecommendedFor: Anyone who isn't deeply comfortable with Haskell and Plutus Core internals.

name: plu-ts
tagline: TypeScript-embedded smart contract language — on-chain and off-chain in one codebase
llmDescription: plu-ts is a TypeScript-embedded smart contract language by Harmonic Labs. It lets you write on-chain validators AND off-chain transaction code in the same TypeScript codebase. It implements its own type system that is checked at JavaScript runtime to produce UPLC. Conceptually, plu-ts is closer to Plutarch (an eDSL) than to Aiken (a standalone language) — you construct Plutus Core terms using TypeScript as the host language. Because it covers both on-chain and off-chain, it is the only tool in the ecosystem that lets you write a complete dApp in a single library without needing a separate SDK. It supports CIP-30 wallet integration. The trade-off is a steeper learning curve and a smaller community than the Aiken + Mesh/Blaze combination.
whenToUse: Use when you want a single TypeScript codebase for both on-chain and off-chain code, and you want fine-grained control over the generated UPLC.
skipUntil: Skip for your first Cardano project. The Aiken + Mesh combination is better documented and has more community support. Consider plu-ts when you've outgrown the standard stack.
recommendedFor: Experienced TypeScript developers who want unified on-chain/off-chain code and fine-grained UPLC control.
notRecommendedFor: Beginners. The learning curve is steeper than Aiken + Mesh, and documentation/tutorials are less abundant.

name: Scalus
tagline: Cardano smart contracts and dApps in Scala 3
llmDescription: Scalus is a Scala 3 implementation of the Cardano Plutus platform. It compiles Scala 3 code to Plutus Core and runs on both JVM and JavaScript platforms. It provides a full-stack Cardano development experience: write smart contracts, build transactions, and application logic all in Scala. Includes a UPLC evaluator for local testing and script evaluation. Scalus is the only option for teams that want to stay entirely in the Scala/JVM ecosystem for both on-chain and off-chain code.
whenToUse: Use when your team works in Scala or the JVM ecosystem and wants to write both on-chain and off-chain code in Scala 3.
skipUntil: Skip unless your team is already in the Scala ecosystem. Aiken + Cardano Client Lib (Java) is the more common JVM path.
recommendedFor: Scala developers who want a unified on-chain/off-chain language on the JVM.
notRecommendedFor: Non-Scala developers. The community is small relative to Aiken or Plinth.

name: Helios
tagline: Lightweight DSL compiling to Plutus Core with JavaScript integration
llmDescription: Helios is a lightweight domain-specific language that compiles to Plutus Core. Its syntax is influenced by JavaScript, making it accessible to web developers. Helios can be used from JavaScript/TypeScript applications and supports Plutus V3. It is simpler and less feature-rich than Aiken — it lacks a built-in package manager, LSP, and property-based testing. Development activity has slowed relative to Aiken, which has become the de facto standard.
whenToUse: Consider if you want a very simple, JavaScript-friendly smart contract language and don't need Aiken's advanced features.
skipUntil: In most cases, prefer Aiken. Helios is mainly relevant for existing projects already using it.
recommendedFor: Existing Helios projects that don't want to migrate.
notRecommendedFor: New projects. Aiken is more actively maintained, better documented, and has broader community support.

name: Marlowe
tagline: Domain-specific language for financial smart contracts with a visual editor
llmDescription: Marlowe is a domain-specific language for financial smart contracts on Cardano. It comes with a visual Playground editor where non-programmers can construct contracts by connecting building blocks. Marlowe is designed for finite, deterministic financial instruments: loans, escrow, swaps, insurance. It is NOT a general-purpose smart contract language — it cannot express arbitrary logic. Marlowe contracts are interpreted by an on-chain Marlowe validator, so the developer doesn't write UPLC directly. The ecosystem includes Marlowe CLI, Marlowe Runtime (application backend), Marlowe Runner (web app for deploying contracts), Marlowe TypeScript SDK, and Marlowe Scan (block explorer). Marlowe contracts are formally analyzable — the Playground can check for common issues before deployment.
whenToUse: Use when building financial instruments (loans, escrow, swaps, options, insurance) and you want a formally verified, auditable contract language — especially if your team includes non-programmers who can use the visual editor.
skipUntil: Skip for anything that isn't a financial contract. Marlowe is purpose-built and cannot express arbitrary on-chain logic like a DEX or DAO.
recommendedFor: Teams building financial products who need formal analysis and visual contract design.
notRecommendedFor: General dApp development. Use Aiken or another general-purpose language instead.

name: Mesh SDK
tagline: The most feature-complete TypeScript SDK for Cardano dApps
llmDescription: Mesh SDK is the most feature-complete TypeScript SDK for Cardano. It provides transaction building, wallet integration (CIP-30 and CIP-95 for governance), NFT minting, staking, governance actions, and React components via @meshsdk/react. Internally it uses the Cardano Serialization Lib (CSL) and CML for CBOR encoding. It supports 7 pluggable providers: Blockfrost, Koios, Maestro, Ogmios, UTxO RPC, Yaci DevKit, and Hydra (beta). It reads CIP-57 blueprints from Aiken, Plinth, and Plutarch. Mesh is used by hundreds of dApps and is the recommended starting point for TypeScript developers. It provides Next.js starter templates ('npx meshjs your-app-name'), extensive documentation at meshjs.dev, and step-by-step guides covering everything from wallet connection to smart contract deployment. It also includes a multi-signature platform at multisig.meshjs.dev.
whenToUse: Use for any TypeScript/JavaScript Cardano dApp. Start here if you're a TypeScript developer — it has the broadest feature set, best documentation, most provider options, and React integration.
skipUntil: You always need an off-chain SDK. You'd skip Mesh only if you specifically need Blaze's lighter footprint or Lucid Evolution's architecture.
recommendedFor: Most TypeScript/JavaScript developers. Largest feature set, best docs, most providers, React components.
notRecommendedFor: Developers who want minimal bundle size or fine-grained control over dependencies (consider Blaze). Non-JS developers.

name: Lucid Evolution
tagline: Modular TypeScript SDK for Cardano — successor to Lucid
llmDescription: Lucid Evolution is the actively maintained successor to the original Lucid library (by SpaceBudz). It is a TypeScript SDK reorganized as a monorepo with modular packages. It uses CML (Cardano Multiplatform Lib) for serialization instead of the older CSL. Supports Plutus V3, Conway-era features, and CIP-57 blueprints. Providers include Blockfrost, Koios, Maestro, and Ogmios. Used in production by major DeFi protocols: Genius Yield, Splash, Ada Markets, Meld, Summon, Strike Finance, Atrium, and others. Does NOT include React components (unlike Mesh). Blueprint schema support is being enhanced as part of a Catalyst-funded initiative aligning with CIP-0057 across Aiken, PlutusTx, and Plutarch.
whenToUse: Use when you prefer a modular SDK architecture, when your project already uses Lucid and you want a smooth migration path, or when one of the DeFi protocols you're integrating with uses it.
skipUntil: Consider Mesh first if starting fresh — it has better documentation and React integration. Lucid Evolution is a strong choice for experienced developers.
recommendedFor: Experienced TypeScript developers, existing Lucid users migrating, teams working with DeFi protocols that use Lucid Evolution.
notRecommendedFor: Beginners who want hand-holding — Mesh has more tutorials and React integration.

name: Blaze
tagline: Lightweight, fast TypeScript SDK for Cardano transactions
llmDescription: Blaze is a lightweight TypeScript SDK for Cardano transaction building, designed for minimal dependencies and fast iteration. It is a monorepo with built-in providers for Blockfrost, Kupmios (Kupo+Ogmios combined), and Maestro. UTxO RPC is available as an external provider package. Blaze does NOT have a built-in Koios provider. Used in production by SundaeSwap, jpg.store, Butane, Fortuna, and Rocket. Blaze is leaner than Mesh — it does not include React components, governance tooling (CIP-95), or as many provider options. It focuses on doing core transaction building well with minimal overhead. It can load CIP-57 blueprint artifacts from Aiken, Plinth, and Plutarch.
whenToUse: Use when you want a lean, fast SDK with minimal dependencies, especially if you're experienced with Cardano and don't need extensive hand-holding or React integration.
skipUntil: Consider Mesh first if you're a beginner, need governance features, or want React components. Blaze is for developers who know what they're doing and want less abstraction.
recommendedFor: Experienced Cardano developers who want minimal abstraction. Projects used by SundaeSwap, jpg.store.
notRecommendedFor: Beginners. Developers who need Koios, governance (CIP-95), or React integration.

name: PyCardano
tagline: Standalone Python library for Cardano — no cardano-cli dependency
llmDescription: PyCardano is a standalone Python library for creating and signing Cardano transactions. It has NO dependency on cardano-cli or external serialization tools — all CBOR encoding is implemented natively in Python, making it lightweight and easy to install in any Python environment. Supports multiple backend providers: Blockfrost (via BlockFrostChainContext), Ogmios (via OgmiosChainContext), Kupo (via KupoChainContextExtension for UTxO pattern matching), and Yaci DevKit (via the pccontext library's YaciDevkitChainContext). PyCardano is OpShin's primary off-chain partner — OpShin-compiled scripts load directly as PyCardano PlutusV3Script objects. It also supports loading Aiken and Plinth compiled artifacts. Covers native script minting, Plutus script interaction, coin selection, fee calculation, and multi-asset transactions.
whenToUse: Use for any Python Cardano project. The only mature Python SDK with standalone serialization. Essential if you're using OpShin for on-chain code.
skipUntil: You always need an off-chain SDK. PyCardano is the default and only real choice for Python.
recommendedFor: All Python developers building on Cardano. The only choice for OpShin off-chain code.
notRecommendedFor: Non-Python developers. Doesn't have browser wallet integration (CIP-30) since Python runs server-side.

name: Cardano Client Lib
tagline: Modular Java SDK for Cardano with pluggable backends
llmDescription: Cardano Client Lib (CCL) is a Java SDK for Cardano by BloxBean. It follows a modular Maven architecture: a core module plus pluggable backend modules for Blockfrost (cardano-client-backend-blockfrost), Koios (cardano-client-backend-koios), and Ogmios/Kupo (cardano-client-backend-ogmios). Each backend is a separate Maven dependency so you include only what you need. CCL supports transaction building, smart contract interaction (loading Plutus scripts), coin selection, native token minting, and account management. It works seamlessly with Yaci DevKit (built by the same BloxBean team) for local testing, and the yaci-cardano-test library provides JUnit 5 integration with testcontainers for automated testing. Also usable from Kotlin and other JVM languages.
whenToUse: Use for any Java or Kotlin Cardano project. The standard and only mature JVM SDK.
skipUntil: You always need an off-chain SDK. CCL is the default for Java/JVM.
recommendedFor: All Java/Kotlin developers building on Cardano. Especially with Yaci DevKit for local testing.
notRecommendedFor: Non-JVM developers.

name: cardano-js-sdk
tagline: Official JavaScript SDK for Cardano by IOG with hardware wallet support
llmDescription: cardano-js-sdk is IOG's official JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for interacting with Cardano. It provides key management with multiple options including hardware wallet support (Ledger, Trezor). It is used internally by the Lace wallet. The SDK is comprehensive but has a larger surface area and more complex API than Mesh or Blaze. It is a potential dependency injection target for Lucid Evolution 2.0. Supports Blockfrost and Ogmios providers.
whenToUse: Use when you need deep hardware wallet integration or when working on wallet development. Also relevant if you're contributing to the Lace ecosystem.
skipUntil: Skip for typical dApp development. Mesh or Blaze are easier to start with and have better dApp-focused documentation.
recommendedFor: Wallet developers, teams needing deep hardware wallet integration, Lace ecosystem contributors.
notRecommendedFor: General dApp developers. Mesh is easier and better documented for most use cases.

name: CardanoSharp
tagline: Cardano cryptographic and serialization library for .NET
llmDescription: CardanoSharp.Wallet is a Cardano cryptographic and serialization library for .NET (C#) applications. It provides transaction building, address derivation, key management, coin selection, minting, and CBOR encoding — all in pure .NET with no native dependencies. It supports Blockfrost and Koios as backend providers. It is the only .NET-native Cardano SDK.
whenToUse: Use for any .NET, C#, or Unity (game engine) Cardano project.
skipUntil: Only relevant if you're building in .NET or Unity.
recommendedFor: All .NET/C# developers. Also relevant for Unity game developers.
notRecommendedFor: Non-.NET developers.

name: CTL (cardano-transaction-lib)
tagline: PureScript library for building Cardano smart contract transactions in the browser
llmDescription: CTL (cardano-transaction-lib) is a PureScript library that ports Plutus off-chain code functionality to the browser and Node.js. It aims to provide a PureScript equivalent of the Haskell off-chain code that traditionally accompanied Plutus on-chain code. CTL uses Ogmios and Kupo as its backend. It supports CIP-30 wallet integration and can build, sign, and submit transactions with Plutus script interaction.
whenToUse: Use when your team works in PureScript or when you want a functional-programming-native off-chain environment that closely mirrors Plutus off-chain conventions.
skipUntil: Skip unless you have specific PureScript expertise or requirements. TypeScript SDKs are far more common.
recommendedFor: PureScript developers, teams migrating from Haskell Plutus off-chain code.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers. PureScript has a small ecosystem compared to TypeScript or Python.

name: Atlas
tagline: Haskell application backend for Plutus smart contracts
llmDescription: Atlas is an application backend by Genius Yield designed to simplify the development of off-chain code for on-chain Plutus smart contracts in Haskell. It supports Maestro and Ogmios+Kupo as backend providers. Atlas is the Haskell-native alternative to using a TypeScript SDK — when your on-chain code is in Plinth or Plutarch, Atlas lets you keep the entire stack in Haskell.
whenToUse: Use when your team is building in Haskell and wants a Haskell-native off-chain backend that integrates directly with Plutus scripts.
skipUntil: Skip if you're not a Haskell team. TypeScript SDKs are far more common for off-chain code.
recommendedFor: Haskell teams who want to keep their entire dApp stack in Haskell.
notRecommendedFor: Non-Haskell developers.

name: Cometa.js
tagline: Fast JavaScript toolkit for Cardano transaction building
llmDescription: Cometa.js is a fast, browser and Node-ready JavaScript toolkit for streamlined Cardano transaction building and dApp smart-contract interactions. It focuses on web and frontend environments with a lightweight footprint.
whenToUse: Consider if you want a JavaScript-native (not TypeScript) alternative to Mesh or Blaze.
skipUntil: Skip for most projects. Mesh and Blaze are more mature and better documented.
recommendedFor: Developers wanting a lightweight JS toolkit as an alternative to larger SDKs.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers. Mesh is the recommended TypeScript/JavaScript starting point.

name: Cometa.py
tagline: High-performance Python library for Cardano automation
llmDescription: Cometa.py is a high-performance Python library for Cardano, focused on rapid prototyping, scripting, and backend automation for transaction building and smart-contract workflows.
whenToUse: Consider as an alternative to PyCardano if you want a different API design.
skipUntil: Skip for most projects. PyCardano is the established Python SDK with broader ecosystem support and OpShin integration.
recommendedFor: Python developers looking for an alternative API to PyCardano.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers. PyCardano is the standard Python SDK.

name: Tx-Village
tagline: Rust/Haskell ecosystem for Cardano smart contract transactions
llmDescription: Tx-Village is MLabs' ecosystem of libraries and tools for working with Cardano smart contract transactions in Rust and Haskell. It provides transaction building, indexing, and submission capabilities for teams working in these languages.
whenToUse: Use when building in Rust or Haskell and you want MLabs' opinionated transaction tooling.
skipUntil: Skip unless you're working with MLabs or specifically need Rust/Haskell transaction libraries.
recommendedFor: Rust/Haskell teams, MLabs ecosystem projects.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers.

name: CML (Cardano Multiplatform Lib)
tagline: Rust/WASM codec library for Cardano data structures — successor to CSL
llmDescription: CML (Cardano Multiplatform Lib) is dcSpark's collection of encoding/decoding codecs for Cardano data structures, intended to replace the older Cardano Serialization Lib (CSL). Serialization code is auto-generated from Cardano's official CDDL specification using cddl-codegen, which guarantees it stays up-to-date. CML provides Rust crates and WASM bindings for browser and Node.js. It includes modules for chain parsing (including multi-era/historical), CIP-25 metadata, and CIP-36. Lucid Evolution uses CML internally. Most dApp developers do NOT use CML directly — it sits beneath higher-level SDKs.
whenToUse: Use when building your own SDK, wallet, or tool that needs direct CBOR serialization/deserialization of Cardano data structures.
skipUntil: Skip unless you're building infrastructure. Off-chain SDKs (Mesh, Lucid Evolution) handle serialization for you.
recommendedFor: SDK developers, wallet developers, infrastructure builders.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers who should use a higher-level SDK instead.

name: Cardano Serialization Lib
tagline: Original Rust/WASM serialization library for Cardano
llmDescription: The Cardano Serialization Lib (CSL) is the original serialization and deserialization library for Cardano's Shelley-era data structures. It provides Rust crates and WASM bindings. CSL is being superseded by CML (Cardano Multiplatform Lib) which is auto-generated from CDDL specs and supports multi-era parsing. Mesh SDK still uses CSL internally. New projects should prefer CML.
whenToUse: Only use if you're maintaining existing code that already depends on CSL.
skipUntil: Prefer CML for new projects. CSL is legacy.
recommendedFor: Legacy project maintenance only.
notRecommendedFor: New projects. Use CML instead.

name: Pallas
tagline: Native Rust reimplementation of Cardano's Ouroboros logic
llmDescription: Pallas is an expanding collection of Rust modules that re-implement common Ouroboros/Cardano logic natively in Rust. It is not a standalone application — it is a foundational base layer for building higher-level tools. Pallas powers TxPipe's entire Rust-based toolchain: Oura (event pipeline), Scrolls (chain indexer), Dolos (data node), and other tools. It provides CBOR parsing, Ouroboros mini-protocol implementations, and chain data types.
whenToUse: Use when building Rust-based Cardano infrastructure: custom indexers, chain followers, data nodes, or protocol-level tools.
skipUntil: Skip unless you're building infrastructure in Rust. dApp developers don't need this directly.
recommendedFor: Rust infrastructure developers building custom Cardano tools.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers. Use an SDK or API provider instead.

name: gOuroboros
tagline: Go implementation of Cardano's Ouroboros network protocols
llmDescription: gOuroboros is a Go implementation of the Cardano Ouroboros protocol family of mini-protocols. It provides the foundation for Go-based Cardano tools, including Dingo (data node), tx-submit-api, and other Blink Labs projects. It enables direct communication with Cardano nodes using both Node-to-Node and Node-to-Client protocols.
whenToUse: Use when building Go-based Cardano infrastructure or tools that need direct node communication.
skipUntil: Skip unless you're building in Go. Pallas is the Rust equivalent.
recommendedFor: Go infrastructure developers.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers. Use an SDK or API provider instead.

name: Blockfrost
tagline: The most widely used Cardano API — free tier, instant setup
llmDescription: Blockfrost is the most widely used Cardano API provider. It offers a REST API that abstracts all node interaction: querying UTxOs, submitting transactions, fetching protocol parameters, account info, asset metadata, pool data, and more. Free tier available (50,000 requests/day). Official SDKs exist for JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust, .NET, Crystal, Kotlin, Elixir, Swift, and more. Blockfrost internally uses Cardano DB Sync for its data. Almost every Cardano off-chain SDK supports Blockfrost as a provider out of the box. Sign up at blockfrost.io for an API key — no credit card required for the free tier.
whenToUse: Use as your first provider. Free tier is generous enough for development and small production apps. Easiest setup — just sign up and paste your API key.
skipUntil: Never skip the provider step — every dApp needs one to query the blockchain. Blockfrost is the default choice.
recommendedFor: Everyone starting out. Best documented, widest SDK support, generous free tier.
notRecommendedFor: Teams that need decentralized infrastructure (consider Koios) or want to self-host everything (consider Ogmios+Kupo).

name: Koios
tagline: Decentralized, community-operated Cardano query layer — no API key needed
llmDescription: Koios is a decentralized, community-operated Cardano query layer. Unlike Blockfrost (centralized, company-operated), Koios runs on multiple redundant endpoints operated by different community members. It is open-source, free to use, and requires no API key. Supported as a provider by Mesh SDK, Cardano Client Lib, and CardanoSharp. Not natively supported by Blaze. Backed by DB Sync on each node.
whenToUse: Use when you want a free, decentralized API with no API key requirement, or when you want to avoid single-vendor dependency.
skipUntil: Consider after you've started with Blockfrost. Koios is a good production alternative for decentralization-minded projects.
recommendedFor: Projects prioritizing decentralization. Production backends wanting provider redundancy.
notRecommendedFor: Teams needing guaranteed SLAs or premium support.

name: Maestro
tagline: Blockchain indexer, APIs, and event management for Cardano
llmDescription: Maestro is a blockchain indexer, API provider, and event management system for Cardano. It offers REST APIs plus webhook-based event streams for real-time monitoring of on-chain activity. Maestro uses its own custom indexer (not DB Sync), which enables specialized queries and event-driven architectures. Supported as a provider by Mesh, Lucid Evolution, Blaze, and Atlas.
whenToUse: Use when you need event-based triggers (webhooks), specialized queries, or a premium managed provider with SLAs.
skipUntil: Use Blockfrost for development. Consider Maestro when you need advanced features, real-time event streams, or production reliability guarantees.
recommendedFor: Production apps needing event-driven architecture, webhooks, or premium SLAs.
notRecommendedFor: Development and prototyping — Blockfrost's free tier is simpler to start with.

name: TangoCrypto
tagline: High-availability APIs and developer tools for Cardano
llmDescription: TangoCrypto provides a suite of high-availability APIs and developer tools for fast, reliable Cardano network access. It offers REST APIs for querying blockchain data and submitting transactions, with SDKs and tooling to reduce development time. It is an alternative to Blockfrost with its own infrastructure.
whenToUse: Consider as an alternative API provider if you need provider diversity or specific features not covered by Blockfrost.
skipUntil: Skip initially. Blockfrost is the standard starting point. TangoCrypto is relevant when diversifying your provider strategy.
recommendedFor: Teams wanting an alternative hosted API provider.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers starting out.

name: Cardano GraphQL
tagline: Typed, queryable GraphQL API for Cardano by the Cardano Foundation
llmDescription: Cardano GraphQL is a cross-platform, typed, and queryable API for Cardano maintained by the Cardano Foundation. It provides a GraphQL schema over Cardano blockchain data (backed by DB Sync) and contains multiple composable packages for building custom GraphQL services. Unlike REST-based providers, GraphQL lets you request exactly the fields you need in a single query.
whenToUse: Use when you prefer GraphQL over REST for querying blockchain data, or when you need complex nested queries that would require multiple REST calls.
skipUntil: Skip initially. REST APIs (Blockfrost, Koios) are simpler and more widely supported by SDKs.
recommendedFor: Teams who prefer GraphQL, complex query requirements.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers. REST APIs are simpler to start with.

name: Cardano Node
tagline: The core node software that runs the Cardano network
llmDescription: The Cardano node is the core software that runs the Cardano network. It implements the Ouroboros consensus protocol, validates and produces blocks, and maintains the ledger state. Every other tool in the ecosystem ultimately connects to a Cardano node — either directly (Ogmios, Kupo, DB Sync) or indirectly via API providers (Blockfrost, Koios, Maestro run their own nodes). Most dApp developers do NOT need to run their own node — API providers handle this. Running a node is required for stake pool operators, infrastructure providers, and self-hosted backend setups.
whenToUse: Run a node if you're operating a stake pool, running a self-hosted backend (Ogmios+Kupo), or building infrastructure tools.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. Use Blockfrost or another API provider, or Yaci DevKit for local testing. You don't need a node on your laptop.
recommendedFor: Stake pool operators, infrastructure providers, self-hosted backend operators.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers who should use API providers or Yaci DevKit instead.

name: Cardano CLI
tagline: Command-line interface for direct Cardano node interaction
llmDescription: cardano-cli is the command-line tool for interacting with a running Cardano node. It can build and sign transactions, manage addresses and keys, query the ledger state, mint tokens, register stake pools, and submit governance actions. It requires a running Cardano node with a local socket connection. cardano-cli is the lowest-level way to interact with Cardano. Most dApp developers use an SDK instead, but cardano-cli is useful for scripting, one-off operations, and understanding how Cardano works at the protocol level.
whenToUse: Use for scripting, DevOps automation, learning how Cardano works at the protocol level, or when you need a quick one-off transaction without writing code.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. Use an SDK instead. cardano-cli is mainly for infrastructure operators and learning.
recommendedFor: Infrastructure operators, DevOps, learning Cardano internals.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers. Use Mesh, PyCardano, etc. instead.

name: Ogmios
tagline: Lightweight WebSocket bridge for Cardano node via JSON-RPC
llmDescription: Ogmios is a lightweight bridge interface sitting in front of a Cardano node. It translates the node's Ouroboros mini-protocols into a JSON-RPC WebSocket API that any programming language can consume. It is NOT a hosted service — you run it yourself alongside your own Cardano node. Ogmios handles transaction submission and evaluation (execution unit calculation), protocol parameter queries, and ledger state queries. For UTxO queries by address or pattern matching, Ogmios is typically paired with Kupo. The Ogmios+Kupo combination is the standard self-hosted alternative to Blockfrost. Supported as a provider by Mesh, Lucid Evolution, Blaze, PyCardano, Cardano Client Lib, CTL, and Atlas.
whenToUse: Use when you want to self-host your backend and avoid third-party API providers. Run Ogmios alongside your own Cardano node, pair with Kupo for UTxO queries.
skipUntil: Skip until you need a self-hosted setup. Blockfrost or Koios are much easier to start with and require zero infrastructure.
recommendedFor: Self-hosted backends, teams wanting full control over infrastructure, privacy-sensitive deployments.
notRecommendedFor: Beginners and teams who don't want to manage infrastructure. Use Blockfrost instead.

name: UTxO RPC
tagline: Standardized gRPC interface for UTxO-based blockchains
llmDescription: UTxO RPC (U5C) is a standardized gRPC interface for UTxO-based blockchains (Cardano and others). It uses Protocol Buffers for a compact, high-performance binary format. Server implementations include Dolos (Rust data node by TxPipe) and Blink Labs' Node API. Client SDKs exist for Rust, Python, Go, Node.js, Deno, and .NET. Supported as a provider by Mesh SDK and Blaze (via external provider package). UTxO RPC's advantage over REST is performance and type safety via gRPC.
whenToUse: Use when you want a high-performance, standardized binary protocol for chain access — especially if you're running Dolos as your data node.
skipUntil: Skip initially. REST APIs (Blockfrost, Koios) are simpler. UTxO RPC is for advanced setups prioritizing performance.
recommendedFor: Performance-sensitive infrastructure, teams running Dolos, cross-chain UTxO development.
notRecommendedFor: Beginners. REST APIs are easier to debug and understand.

name: Dingo
tagline: Cardano data node written in Go
llmDescription: Dingo is a Cardano blockchain data node written in Go by Blink Labs. It participates in network communications using the Ouroboros Node-to-Node mini-protocols (powered by gOuroboros). Dingo is a lightweight alternative to running a full Cardano node when you only need to follow the chain and serve data.
whenToUse: Use when you need a lightweight Go-based chain follower, especially in Go infrastructure projects.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. This is infrastructure tooling.
recommendedFor: Go infrastructure developers who need a lightweight chain follower.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers.

name: Mithril
tagline: Fast node bootstrapping and lightweight blockchain certification
llmDescription: Mithril is a protocol for fast node bootstrapping and secure, lightweight blockchain certification. Instead of syncing a Cardano node from genesis (which can take hours), Mithril provides certified snapshots that allow a node to bootstrap in minutes. It uses stake-based threshold signatures for security. Mithril also enables light client verification — applications can verify blockchain data without running a full node. Essential for efficient node operations and scaling infrastructure.
whenToUse: Use when you need to sync a Cardano node quickly, or when building light clients that need to verify chain data without a full node.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. Mithril is infrastructure — relevant when you're operating nodes or building light client applications.
recommendedFor: Node operators who need fast sync, light client developers.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers using API providers.

name: Oura
tagline: Rust pipeline for streaming Cardano blockchain events
llmDescription: Oura is a Rust-native stream processing pipeline for Cardano. It connects to the tip of a Cardano node (via Ouroboros mini-protocols or UTxO RPC) and emits structured events to external systems like Kafka, Elasticsearch, Redis, webhooks, or files. Built on Pallas. Oura is for real-time event processing — it doesn't store data itself, but streams it to your preferred sink.
whenToUse: Use when you need real-time event streaming from the Cardano chain to external systems (Kafka, Elasticsearch, webhooks, etc.).
skipUntil: Skip for typical dApp development. Oura is for building custom data pipelines and event-driven architectures.
recommendedFor: Infrastructure teams building real-time data pipelines, event-driven architectures.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers. Use API providers for querying data.

name: Dolos
tagline: Lightweight Rust data node for Cardano with UTxO RPC
llmDescription: Dolos is a trimmed-down, Rust-based Cardano 'data node' built by TxPipe on top of Pallas. Unlike the full Cardano node, Dolos only serves data — it syncs from upstream relay nodes and keeps a local copy of the chain. It uses UTxO RPC as its main interface for client integration. Dolos is lighter than a full node and is designed as a UTxO RPC server.
whenToUse: Use when you want a lightweight chain-following node that exposes UTxO RPC, especially if you're building on the TxPipe stack.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. This is infrastructure tooling for teams running their own backend stack.
recommendedFor: Teams wanting a lightweight UTxO RPC server without running a full Cardano node.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers.

name: tx-submit-api
tagline: Simple HTTP API for submitting Cardano transactions
llmDescription: tx-submit-api is a simple HTTP API by Blink Labs that accepts CBOR-encoded Cardano transactions and submits them to a Cardano node using the Ouroboros LocalTxSubmission protocol. It is a minimal, focused tool that does one thing: submit transactions. Useful for custom infrastructure setups where you only need transaction submission without a full API provider.
whenToUse: Use when you only need transaction submission in a custom infrastructure setup.
skipUntil: Skip — API providers and Ogmios handle transaction submission as part of broader functionality.
recommendedFor: Custom infrastructure setups needing a minimal tx submission endpoint.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers. Use your SDK's built-in submission via a provider.

name: Cardano DB Sync
tagline: Core chain indexer storing all blockchain data in PostgreSQL
llmDescription: Cardano DB Sync is one of Cardano's core components. It follows the Cardano chain and stores all blockchain data (blocks, transactions, UTxOs, metadata, staking, governance) in a PostgreSQL database. It is the most comprehensive indexer — it indexes everything. API providers like Blockfrost and Koios use DB Sync internally. The trade-off is resource-heaviness: DB Sync requires significant disk space (hundreds of GB), RAM, and time to sync from genesis. Most dApp developers never run DB Sync directly.
whenToUse: Use when you need complete, unrestricted SQL access to all Cardano blockchain data — for analytics, custom queries, or building your own API layer.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. API providers (which use DB Sync internally) are much easier. Only run DB Sync if you need custom SQL queries over the entire chain.
recommendedFor: Analytics platforms, custom API providers, teams needing unrestricted SQL access to chain data.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers. Extremely resource-intensive to run.

name: Yaci Store
tagline: Modular Java-based Cardano indexer with Blockfrost-compatible APIs
llmDescription: Yaci Store is a modular, high-performance Cardano blockchain indexer and datastore by BloxBean (same team as Cardano Client Lib and Yaci DevKit). Built on top of the Yaci Java library, it offers a plugin framework for custom extensions. It exposes Blockfrost-compatible API endpoints, meaning SDKs configured for Blockfrost can point to Yaci Store with no code changes. It is the built-in indexer inside Yaci DevKit. Lighter than DB Sync, uses H2 or PostgreSQL.
whenToUse: Use when you need a lightweight, pluggable Java-based indexer — especially if you're already in the BloxBean/Yaci ecosystem.
skipUntil: Skip unless you need to run your own indexer. Most devs use API providers. You get Yaci Store automatically with Yaci DevKit for local development.
recommendedFor: Java teams needing a custom indexer, Yaci DevKit users.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers who should use API providers.

name: Kupo
tagline: Lightweight, pattern-matching chain-index for Cardano
llmDescription: Kupo is a lightweight, configurable chain-index for Cardano. Unlike full indexers like DB Sync (which index everything), Kupo lets you specify patterns to match — specific addresses, policy IDs, output references, etc. — so it only stores what you need. This makes it very fast and resource-efficient. Kupo is typically paired with Ogmios to provide a complete self-hosted backend: Ogmios handles tx submission and evaluation, Kupo handles UTxO queries by pattern. This Ogmios+Kupo combination is supported by Blaze (as 'Kupmios'), CTL, Atlas, PyCardano, and Cardano Client Lib.
whenToUse: Use alongside Ogmios when you're self-hosting and need UTxO queries for specific addresses or policies. The Ogmios+Kupo pair is the standard self-hosted backend.
skipUntil: Skip until you need a self-hosted setup. Blockfrost indexes everything for you and requires no infrastructure.
recommendedFor: Self-hosted backends paired with Ogmios. Resource-constrained environments where DB Sync is too heavy.
notRecommendedFor: Beginners. Use Blockfrost instead.

name: Scrolls
tagline: Read-optimized chain data collections via map/reduce over Cardano history
llmDescription: Scrolls is a TxPipe tool that builds and maintains read-optimized collections of Cardano's on-chain entities. It crawls the chain history and aggregates data to reflect the current state using a map/reduce approach. Once synced, Scrolls watches the tip to keep collections up-to-date. It outputs to Redis, Elasticsearch, or other storage backends. Built on Pallas and supports UTxO RPC as an input protocol.
whenToUse: Use when you need custom read-optimized views of chain data in Redis, Elasticsearch, or similar stores.
skipUntil: Skip for typical dApp development. Scrolls is for building custom data views and analytics.
recommendedFor: Teams building custom analytics or search over chain data.
notRecommendedFor: dApp developers who should use API providers.

name: Carp
tagline: Modular Rust/PostgreSQL indexer for Cardano by dcSpark
llmDescription: Carp is dcSpark's modular indexer that syncs Cardano blockchain data to a PostgreSQL database. It uses a task-based architecture where each indexing task is a separate module. Built on Pallas for chain parsing. Lighter than DB Sync with a more modular design.
whenToUse: Use when you need a modular Rust-based indexer with PostgreSQL output and want more control over what gets indexed.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. Use API providers or Yaci Store.
recommendedFor: Rust infrastructure teams, dcSpark ecosystem projects.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers.

name: Ledger Sync
tagline: Cardano Foundation's Java-based chain synchronization tool
llmDescription: Ledger Sync is the Cardano Foundation's Java-based chain synchronization tool for indexing Cardano blockchain data. It provides structured data indexing as an alternative to DB Sync, built in Java for teams in the JVM ecosystem.
whenToUse: Use when you need a Java-based indexer from the Cardano Foundation.
skipUntil: Skip for dApp development. Use API providers or Yaci Store.
recommendedFor: Java infrastructure teams, Cardano Foundation ecosystem projects.
notRecommendedFor: Most developers.

name: Yaci DevKit
tagline: Complete local Cardano devnet in seconds — the standard local development tool
llmDescription: Yaci DevKit provides a complete local Cardano development environment that can be created and destroyed in seconds. It runs a local Cardano node, the Yaci Store indexer, and a web-based block viewer. It supports sub-second block times (down to 100ms) for ultra-fast iteration. Crucially, it exposes Blockfrost-compatible API endpoints, meaning any SDK configured for Blockfrost can connect to Yaci DevKit by just changing the provider URL to localhost — zero code changes required. Available as Docker Compose, ZIP distribution, or NPM package ('npm install -g @bloxbean/yaci-devkit'). It also integrates Ogmios and Kupo for SDKs that use those backends. Yaci DevKit supports rollback testing via multi-node setups, configurable genesis parameters, and address top-up via CLI or admin API. Built by the same BloxBean team behind Cardano Client Lib and Yaci Store.
whenToUse: Use from day one of development. Spin up a local devnet instead of using the public testnet — it's instant, free, resettable, and doesn't require waiting for block confirmations. Essential for CI/CD pipelines.
skipUntil: Never skip local development tooling. Yaci DevKit is the standard choice.
recommendedFor: Every Cardano developer. Works with all major SDKs. Essential for productive iteration and CI/CD.
notRecommendedFor: Nobody — every developer should use local devnets.

name: Yaci Cardano Test
tagline: Java testing library with testcontainers for automated Cardano testing
llmDescription: Yaci Cardano Test is a Java library that provides programmatic Cardano testing using testcontainers. It starts a Yaci DevKit container in Docker programmatically, funds test addresses, and provides Cardano-specific JUnit 5 assertions. It integrates with Cardano Client Lib for transaction building. The Yaci Store API (Blockfrost-compatible or Ogmios mode) is accessible from test code.
whenToUse: Use when writing automated Java/JUnit 5 tests for Cardano applications. The natural testing companion for Cardano Client Lib.
skipUntil: Only relevant for Java developers who want automated integration testing with a real devnet.
recommendedFor: Java developers using Cardano Client Lib who want JUnit-based integration tests.
notRecommendedFor: Non-Java developers.

name: Mockfrost
tagline: Mock Blockfrost API for Python smart contract testing
llmDescription: Mockfrost (formerly Plutus Bench) creates a mock ledger with an arbitrary UTxO set and hosts a mock Blockfrost API for testing. It supports PyCardano and other tools that use the Blockfrost API format. Currently in beta. Useful for unit testing smart contract interactions without a real devnet.
whenToUse: Use for lightweight Python-based testing of smart contract interactions when you don't need a full devnet.
skipUntil: Skip until you need fast unit tests in Python. Yaci DevKit is more comprehensive for integration testing.
recommendedFor: Python developers wanting fast, lightweight smart contract unit tests.
notRecommendedFor: Developers needing full integration testing (use Yaci DevKit).

name: Hydra Head
tagline: Layer 2 isomorphic state channels for Cardano — near-instant transactions
llmDescription: Hydra is Cardano's primary Layer 2 scaling solution, using isomorphic state channels called 'Hydra Heads.' Each head is an off-chain mini-ledger shared between a small group of participants where transactions are near-instant and low-cost. The isomorphic design means Hydra Heads support the same features as Cardano mainnet — native tokens, NFTs, and Plutus smart contracts work in a head without modification. The final state is settled on the main chain. Hydra has been on mainnet since May 2023 (version 0.10.0+). Mesh SDK has a beta Hydra provider. Hydra is ideal for high-throughput use cases: gaming, micropayments, auctions, point-of-sale, and collaborative tools. It achieves linear scalability — each new head adds capacity.
whenToUse: Use when your application needs high transaction throughput, low latency, or low per-transaction cost — gaming, micropayments, auctions, real-time collaborative tools, point-of-sale systems.
skipUntil: Skip for your first dApp. Hydra adds complexity. Start on Layer 1 and move to Hydra when you hit scalability limits or have a specific use case that demands it.
recommendedFor: High-throughput dApps: gaming, micropayments, auctions, point-of-sale, collaborative tools.
notRecommendedFor: First-time dApp developers. Standard DeFi or NFT projects that work fine on Layer 1.

name: Charli3
tagline: Decentralized oracle network for Cardano with inter-node vetoing
llmDescription: Charli3 is an open-source, decentralized network of oracles purpose-built for Cardano. It brings off-chain data (prices, events, etc.) onto the blockchain using inter-node vetoing to ensure data accuracy. Charli3 provides the Python SDK 'charli3-dendrite' for DeFi developers to interact with multiple Cardano DEXs using data from Charli3 oracles.
whenToUse: Use when your smart contract needs real-world data — price feeds, exchange rates, weather data, sports results, or any external data source.
skipUntil: Skip unless your smart contract needs off-chain data. Most NFT, governance, and simple payment dApps don't need oracles.
recommendedFor: DeFi protocols needing price feeds, prediction markets, insurance dApps.
notRecommendedFor: Projects that don't need off-chain data in their smart contracts.

name: Orcfax
tagline: Decentralized Cardano oracle publishing real-world data for smart contracts
llmDescription: Orcfax is a decentralized oracle for Cardano that publishes authenticated real-world data for smart contracts. It focuses on data provenance and auditability — each data point includes a full audit trail. Orcfax is an alternative to Charli3 with a different approach to data sourcing and verification.
whenToUse: Use when your smart contract needs real-world data and you prioritize data provenance and audit trails.
skipUntil: Skip unless you need oracle data. Consider alongside Charli3 based on your specific data needs.
recommendedFor: DeFi protocols, enterprise applications requiring auditable oracle data.
notRecommendedFor: Projects that don't need off-chain data.

name: NMKR Studio
tagline: Comprehensive platform for Cardano NFT minting, burning, and management
llmDescription: NMKR Studio is a comprehensive platform for Cardano NFT management built with C# and .NET. It provides backend tools and services for minting, burning, and managing NFTs. It offers white-label solutions for brands, companies, and creators. NMKR handles the infrastructure complexity so creators can focus on content. It includes APIs for programmatic access and a web dashboard for no-code NFT management.
whenToUse: Use when you need a managed, no-code/low-code NFT minting and management platform — especially for brands, artists, or companies that don't want to write code.
skipUntil: Skip if your SDK (Mesh, PyCardano, etc.) has enough built-in minting capabilities for your needs. NMKR is mainly for specialized NFT workflows.
recommendedFor: Brands, artists, and companies needing managed NFT infrastructure without coding.
notRecommendedFor: Developers who prefer minting via code using their SDK.

name: Demeter.run
tagline: Cloud platform with managed Cardano infrastructure for dApp development
llmDescription: Demeter.run is a cloud-based platform by TxPipe equipped with all necessary resources for building Cardano dApps. It provides managed Cardano nodes, Ogmios instances, Kupo instances, and development environments accessible via a web browser. It offers extensions that you can add to your project (Ogmios, Kupo, DB Sync, etc.) without managing any infrastructure. The OpShin starter kit can be opened directly in Demeter.run for instant development.
whenToUse: Use when you want to start developing immediately without installing anything locally, or when you need managed Cardano infrastructure in the cloud.
skipUntil: Skip if you prefer local development with Yaci DevKit. Demeter.run is most useful for quick prototyping, hackathons, or teams that want zero infrastructure management.
recommendedFor: Hackathon participants, teams wanting zero-setup cloud development, OpShin users.
notRecommendedFor: Teams that prefer local development or need offline capability.

name: Plutonomy
tagline: Optimizer for Untyped Plutus Core — reduce script size and execution costs
llmDescription: Plutonomy is an optimizer for Untyped Plutus Core (UPLC) that can be plugged into any Plutus compilation pipeline as an extra pass. It applies various optimization strategies to reduce script size and execution costs. There is also a CLI wrapper (plutonomy-cli) for command-line usage. Relevant for Plinth and Plutarch users who want to optimize their compiled UPLC before deployment.
whenToUse: Use when you have a compiled Plutus script (from Plinth or Plutarch) and want to optimize its size and execution costs before deploying to mainnet.
skipUntil: Skip until you're optimizing production smart contracts. Not needed during development or for Aiken (which has its own optimizer).
recommendedFor: Plinth and Plutarch users deploying to production who need to reduce script costs.
notRecommendedFor: Aiken users (Aiken optimizes internally). Beginners.

name: Liqwid-libs
tagline: Plutarch helper libraries for advanced Plutus smart contract development
llmDescription: Liqwid-libs is a monorepo of Plutarch helper libraries maintained by Liqwid Labs. It provides reusable patterns, utility functions, and abstractions for building complex Plutus smart contracts using the Plutarch eDSL. Used in production by the Liqwid lending protocol.
whenToUse: Use when building complex Plutarch smart contracts and you want battle-tested helper functions and patterns from the Liqwid team.
skipUntil: Skip unless you're using Plutarch. These are Plutarch-specific helper libraries.
recommendedFor: Plutarch developers building complex DeFi contracts.
notRecommendedFor: Anyone not using Plutarch.

name: Cardanoscan
tagline: Feature-rich Cardano blockchain explorer and analytics platform
llmDescription: Cardanoscan is a feature-rich blockchain explorer and analytics platform for Cardano. It provides detailed information about transactions, addresses, tokens, NFTs, stake pools, governance actions, and smart contract interactions. It supports both mainnet and testnets. It is the most commonly recommended explorer for debugging transactions during development and monitoring in production.
whenToUse: Use from day one. You'll use an explorer constantly to inspect transactions, verify contract interactions, check token metadata, and debug issues.
skipUntil: Never skip — every developer needs a block explorer.
recommendedFor: Everyone. The primary tool for inspecting and debugging Cardano transactions.
notRecommendedFor: Nobody.

name: Cexplorer
tagline: The oldest and most featured explorer on the Cardano network
llmDescription: Cexplorer (formerly AdaPools) is the oldest and most featured explorer on the Cardano network. It provides comprehensive analytics on governance, pools, tokens, transactions, and network health. Particularly strong for stake pool analytics and governance tracking.
whenToUse: Use alongside or instead of Cardanoscan, especially for stake pool analytics and governance data.
skipUntil: Optional — Cardanoscan covers most needs. Cexplorer is a good complement.
recommendedFor: Stake pool operators, governance participants, analytics researchers.
notRecommendedFor: Nobody — it's a useful complement to any explorer.
